name: 7-Hydroxymitragynine
summary: 7-Hydroxymitragynine is a potent kratom alkaloid present only in trace amounts in natural kratom leaf, but concentrated or synthetic 7-OH products carry opioid-like safety and regulatory risk.
description: 7-Hydroxymitragynine (7-OH) is a kratom alkaloid with mu-opioid receptor activity. The 2025-2026 regulatory focus is concentrated, synthetic, enhanced, or added-ingredient 7-OH products such as tablets, gummies, shots, and enhanced extracts, not plain natural kratom leaf with trace 7-OH.
interactions: check medication and supplement interactions before use.
conditions: not enough human evidence for a consumer recommendation
buying_criteria: Do not promote or source concentrated/synthetic 7-OH products
distinguish natural kratom leaf from enhanced or added-ingredient 7-OH products
verify legal status and testing disclosures.
legal_status: FDA enforcement concern; DEA scheduling recommendation under review as of June 2026; state and local rules vary.
controlled_status: FDA recommended Schedule I control for certain concentrated/synthetic 7-OH products; DEA final action pending as of June 2026.
dea_status: DEA reviewing FDA scheduling recommendation; no final CSA scheduling action confirmed as of June 2026.
regulatory_status: Federal scheduling recommended for certain concentrated/synthetic 7-OH products; no final DEA scheduling as of June 2026.
regulatory_federal: FDA announced on July 29, 2025 that it recommended CSA Schedule I control for certain concentrated 7-hydroxymitragynine products, including tablets, gummies, shots, drink mixes, and enhanced extracts. FDA explicitly distinguished this action from natural kratom leaf, where 7-OH is a trace alkaloid that is typically less than 2% of total alkaloids.|FDA warning letters in June/July 2025 targeted firms marketing concentrated 7-OH products. FDA states that 7-OH is not lawful in dietary supplements, cannot be lawfully added to conventional foods, and is not present in any FDA-approved drug.|On December 2, 2025, FDA and DOJ announced U.S. Marshals seizures of about 73,000 units of concentrated 7-OH products valued around $1 million from Missouri firms. As of this June 2026 workbook check, DEA review was still treated as pending; no final federal scheduling rule is represented here.
regulatory_states_summary: State rules are a patchwork. Many KCPA-style frameworks distinguish natural kratom leaf from enhanced products through age gates, labeling, contaminant testing, and limits such as 7-OH not exceeding about 2% of total alkaloids. Some states or localities ban kratom outright; others have active or recently changed 7-OH-specific proposals. Verify official state and local sources because this area is changing rapidly.
